title: Treehouse Bed
summary: A twin bed frame built to look like a peaked-roof treehouse cabin, with real board-and-batten siding and a shingled roof — a genuine themed structure, not just a decorated bed frame.
description:
This is a real structure built around a twin mattress rather than a themed headboard bolted onto a plain frame — angled 2x4 legs form a peaked roofline, sided in 1x8 boards, and roofed with overlapping 1x8 roofing boards laid working out from the center. Lay out the front and back frames first, being precise about the roof angles at the top of the legs and confirming both the top and bottom of the legs measure exactly 75" wide — **this width has to match at both top and bottom**, or the whole structure develops a lean once the roof trim and siding go on.

Once both end frames stand, the roof-angled trim ties them together, and the remaining roof trim attaches to the fronts of the legs — **keep the front roof trim low enough that the roofing boards added later will lay flat along the marked line**, a detail easy to get wrong if you eyeball the trim height instead of checking it against the plan's reference line.

Siding goes on starting from the bottom row and working up, checking square and level as you go, since an early siding board that's slightly off will compound by the time you reach the roofline. The roof itself is shingled from the center out — **work from the center roofing board outward in both directions**, screwing carefully into the front 1x6 roofing trim underneath, since a screw that misses this trim board won't hold. Mattress slats go in last, spaced roughly 5" apart, with the plan explicitly noting to add more slats than shown if your particular mattress needs firmer support.

materials.name: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

cutList.note: Long point measurement, one end cut at 10° bevel.
Long point measurement, one end cut at 10° bevel.
Long point to short point, both ends cut 10° off square, ends parallel.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut two back legs at 75", three front legs at 67-7/8", two mattress cleats at 75", one ladder step at 31-3/4" and nine mattress slats at 39" from the 2x4, 8 ft or stud length. Cut two side roof trim at 42-5/8" and two front/back roof trim at 76-1/2" from the 1x6. Cut eight side siding at 42", four front siding at 47-1/2", four small front siding at 4-1/4", one front mattress trim at 24-3/4" and eleven roof boards at 45-3/4" from the 1x8. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to twelve 8 ft 2x4, 8 ft or stud length, three 8 ft 1x6 and twelve 8 ft 1x8. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Build the front and back frames
steps.body: Build two frames, each 75" wide: a back frame from the two 75" back legs and a front frame from the three 67-7/8" front legs. The 10° bevel goes at the top of every leg, all sloping the same way, and the 7-1/8" difference between the 75" and 67-7/8" legs is exactly what produces that slope once the frames stand. Screw a 75" mattress cleat across the inside faces of each frame's legs with 2-3/4" screws, setting both cleats at the same height and checking each is level so the mattress lies flat. Before the last screws go in, measure across the tops of the legs and across the bottoms: both readings must be exactly 75", or the whole structure develops a lean the moment the roof trim and siding add weight.

steps.title: Join the frames with roof trim
steps.body: Stand both frames upright and parallel with the taller back frame behind the shorter front frame, then tie them together at the top with the two 42-5/8" side roof trim pieces, one down each side. Each is cut long point to short point with both ends 10° off square and parallel, so it beds flat on the beveled leg tops and sets the bed's front-to-back depth at the same time. Drive 2-3/4" screws through each trim board into the top of every leg it crosses. Before moving on, check that the gap between the two frames measures the same at both ends and that each frame is still square — everything from here registers off this box.

steps.title: Add the remaining roof trim
steps.body: Attach the two 76-1/2" front/back roof trim boards across the fronts of the front legs and across the back frame. At 76-1/2" they run 3/4" past the 75" frame width at each end, which covers the cut ends of the side roof trim and closes the roofline in. Set the front board low enough that a roof board laid on the 10° slope bears flat along it instead of riding up over its top edge — lay one roof board across it and sight along the slope before you commit, because this is the detail that goes wrong when the trim height is eyeballed. Fasten with 2-3/4" screws driven into the legs behind, not just into the side trim.

steps.title: Attach the siding
steps.body: Start with the bottom row of side siding — eight 42" boards, four per side, each the 1x8's full 7-1/4" width — setting the first board level along the bottom of the legs and driving 1-3/4" screws into every leg it crosses. Work upward from there and check level on every row: a first row that sits 1/8" out of level compounds into a visibly wedged gap by the time you reach the roofline. Side the front the same way with the four 47-1/2" front siding boards, butting the edges tight rather than spacing them. Cut the four 4-1/4" small front siding pieces last, from your offcuts, to fill the short gaps left around the opening; run any board long and trim it flush afterward rather than trying to land a cut exactly.

steps.title: Add the mattress trim and ladder step
steps.body: Screw the 24-3/4" front mattress trim across the front opening at the height of the mattress cleats with 1-3/4" screws, so the mattress cannot slide out through the opening. Then mount the 31-3/4" ladder step across the front legs, centering it vertically between the floor and the mattress trim so the climb up is one even reach. Fasten the step with 2-3/4" screws driven into the solid 2x4 legs behind the siding — this piece takes a child's full weight, and screws that stop in 3/4" siding will not hold it.

steps.title: Attach the roof
steps.body: Find the middle of the 75" roofline and set the first of the eleven 45-3/4" roof boards there, running it front to back down the slope, then screw it with 1-3/4" screws into the front 1x6 roof trim underneath. Work outward from that center board in both directions, adding one board to each side in turn and overlapping each new board onto the one before so water and dust shed down the slope. Every screw along the front edge has to land in that 1x6 trim — a screw that misses it holds nothing — so mark the trim's position on the board faces before you start driving. Eleven 7-1/4" boards overlapped cover the 75" length with room to spare, so adjust the overlap a little as you go rather than leaving one wide gap at an end.

steps.title: Add the mattress slats
steps.body: Drop the nine 39" mattress slats across the bed onto the two cleats and space them about 5" apart along the 75" length, working from the middle outward so any leftover space lands at the ends. Screw each slat end down into the cleat with a 1-3/4" screw so nothing shifts when the bed is climbed on. Nine slats is a minimum, not a maximum: a thin or foam-only mattress wants closer spacing, and adding a few more is the cheapest insurance against a sagging middle.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Sand every surface a child will touch and ease the sharp corners, paying particular attention to the ladder step, the front mattress trim and the leg edges around the opening. Prime and paint, or stain and seal with poly, working the finish into the siding overlaps where bare edges would otherwise show. Let the finish cure fully before the mattress and bedding go in.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

images.alt: Twin bed built to look like a peaked-roof treehouse cabin
